diagrams.title: Basic Page Table Structure and Address Translation
diagrams.description: Shows how a virtual address is translated to a physical address using a page table, illustrating the page number and offset components
elements: Virtual Address box split into Page Number (left portion) and Offset (right portion)
Page Table box with multiple entries indexed by page number
Each PTE shows Frame Number and control bits (valid bit, protection bits)
Physical Address box showing Frame Number concatenated with Offset
Arrow from Virtual Address Page Number pointing to corresponding Page Table entry
Arrow from PTE Frame Number to Physical Address Frame Number portion
MMU (Memory Management Unit) as processing unit between virtual and physical addresses
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the fundamental mechanism of virtual-to-physical address translation and understand the role of page tables in this process

diagrams.title: Two-Level Hierarchical Page Table
diagrams.description: Illustrates a two-level page table structure where the first-level page table points to second-level page tables, reducing memory overhead
elements: Outer Page Table (Level 1) with entries pointing to inner page tables
Multiple Inner Page Tables (Level 2) for different address space regions
Valid/Invalid bits in outer page table entries (shaded for invalid regions)
Virtual Address divided into: P1 (outer page index), P2 (inner page index), Offset
Arrows showing hierarchical lookup: Virtual Addr → Outer PT → Inner PT → Physical Frame
Shaded regions indicating unused address space portions
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how hierarchical page tables conserve memory by allocating only needed second-level tables and marking unused regions as invalid

diagrams.title: Inverted Page Table Structure
diagrams.description: Shows an inverted page table where entries are indexed by physical frame number rather than virtual page number
elements: Inverted Page Table array with one entry per physical frame
Each entry contains: Frame Number, Process ID, Virtual Page Number
Hash table column adjacent to inverted table for faster lookup
Virtual Address showing Process ID, Virtual Page Number, Offset
Hashing function box converting VPN to hash bucket index
Chain of entries in each hash bucket showing collision handling
diagrams.purpose: Students learn how inverted page tables reduce memory overhead in large address spaces and understand the hashing mechanism used for fast lookup

diagrams.title: Hashed Page Table
diagrams.description: Demonstrates a hashed page table structure commonly used for large address spaces with virtual page numbers hashed to table indices
elements: Hashed Page Table array with buckets/chains
Each bucket entry contains: Virtual Page Number, Mapped Frame Number, Pointer to next entry
Hash function taking Virtual Page Number as input
Virtual Address components: Virtual Page Number, Offset
Arrows showing: VPN → Hash Function → Bucket Index → Traverse Chain → Find Frame
Collision handling shown as linked list chains at each bucket
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how hashed page tables handle sparse address spaces efficiently and manage collisions through chaining

diagrams.title: Page Table Entry (PTE) Format
diagrams.description: Shows the detailed bit-level structure of a typical page table entry with all control and address fields
elements: 32-bit or 64-bit PTE divided into bit fields
Fields: Valid bit (1 bit), Modified/Dirty bit (1 bit), Referenced bit (1 bit), Protection bits (3 bits)
Frame Number field (remaining bits after control bits)
Optional fields: Cache disable bit, Access bit, User/Supervisor bit
Visual distinction between control bits (shaded) and frame number field
Arrows showing what each control bit represents
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the internal structure of page table entries and understand the purpose of each control bit in memory management

charts.title: Comparison of Page Table Structures
charts.content: Single-level: Simple but wastes memory for sparse addresses | Two-level: Reduces memory, adds lookup overhead | Inverted: Saves memory for large systems, slower lookup | Hashed: Good for sparse address spaces, handles collisions via chains | Inverted with hashing: Combines benefits of both, used in modern systems

charts.title: Page Table Entry (PTE) Field Descriptions
charts.content: Valid Bit: Indicates if page is in physical memory | Frame Number: Physical frame where page is located | Protection Bits: Read/Write/Execute permissions | Modified (Dirty) Bit: Shows if page was modified | Referenced (Access) Bit: Tracks recent page access | Cache Disable: Prevents caching of page

charts.title: Address Translation Process Flow
charts.content: Start → Extract Page Number from Virtual Address → Check Valid Bit in PTE → If Invalid: Page Fault → If Valid: Extract Frame Number → Combine with Offset → Form Physical Address → Access Memory → End

charts.title: Contiguous vs Non-Contiguous Memory Allocation
charts.content: Contiguous: Simple allocation, suffers from external fragmentation, limited to available contiguous blocks | Non-Contiguous (Paging): Eliminates external fragmentation, allows non-contiguous physical allocation, requires page tables, introduces internal fragmentation

mindMaps.centralConcept: Page Table Structure in Memory Management
branches: Components: Page Number field, Offset field, Frame Number, Control bits
Control Bits: Valid/Invalid, Protection, Modified, Referenced
Address Translation: MMU performs lookup, TLB caching speeds up translation
Memory Overhead: Trade-off between table size and lookup speed
Advantages: Enables virtual memory, protection, sharing, non-contiguous allocation
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview of page table concepts, structures, and their role in operating system memory management for engineering students

branches: Virtual Address Space: Range of addresses accessible to process
Physical Memory: Actual RAM divided into frames
Page Table: Mapping between virtual and physical pages
Translation: Virtual Page Number → Frame Number
Offset: Unchanged during translation (page offset same)
Page Fault: Occurs when valid bit is 0 (page not in memory)
Page Replacement: When memory is full, evict victim page to disk
mindMaps.purpose: Helps students connect page table structure to the broader concept of virtual memory and understand the complete address translation workflow
